content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance     Administration       Executive/Legal     Enforcement       Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5660         322-5660             322-5901            322-6441           322-6444

February 10, 1982


Harvey Levine, City Attorney
City of Pleasanton
P.O. Box 520
Pleasanton, CA  94566

Dear Mr. Levine:

     I am writing in response to your question to Alice Hughes
regarding the reportability of a loan to a business entity.¹
The staff has reviewed this question, and has determined that
the name of a lender to a business entity must be reported on an
official's Statement of Economic Interests if:

     (1)  The official has a direct or indirect interest of
10 percent or more in the business entity; and

     (2)  The official's pro rata share of the loan is
$10,000 or more.

     Under the Political Reform Act, the term income includes
all payments received, including loans.  In addition, the pro rata
share of any income of a business entity in which an official
has a 10 percent or greater interest is income to the official,²
within the meaning of the Act.  Government Code Section 82030.²
Thus a pro rata share both of the total gross income of a busi-
ness entity in which an official has a 10 percent or greater
interest, and of any loan or other payment to the business entity,
is income to the official.


     An official has to report his or her pro rata share of
income to a business entity in two ways.  First, the official
has to report a pro rata share of the total gross income (including

_______________________________
  1/If the official is filing under a conflict of interest
code, I am assuming that investments in and income from this
type of business entity is reportable under the Code.

  2/All statutory references are to the Government Code unless
otherwise stated.

Harvey Levine, City Attorney
February 10, 1982
Page Two


loans) of the business entity as income on Schedule D.  Second,
under Section 87207(b), the official has to report the name of
each person who makes payment to a business entity if the official's
pro rata share of the payment is $10,000 or more.  Since the
term "payment" is explicitly defined to include a loan, Section
82030, and since the term "person" includes institutions, such
as banks, which make loans, Section 82047, an official has to
report a loan to a business entity under this section if his or
her pro rata share of the loan is $10,000 or more.  This should
be reported on Schedule H by officials filing Form 721, and on
Schedule G by officials filing Form 730.

I hope this answers your questions.  If you have any further
questions about the disclosure requirements of the Act, please
feel free to contact either Ms. Hughes (916/322-6444) or me
(916/322-5901) for advice.

Very truly yours,

Barbara A. Milman
Chief
Legal Division

BAM:plh

sections.question_synthetic: Must an official report the name of a lender to a business entity on their Statement of Economic Interests, and under what conditions?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: An official must report a lender's name on their Statement of Economic Interests if the official has a direct or indirect interest of 10% or more in the business entity AND the official's pro rata share of the loan is $10,000 or more. The loan should be reported on Schedule H (Form 721) or Schedule G (Form 730), and the pro rata share of the loan income must also be reported on Schedule D.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: No formal QUESTION or CONCLUSION section headers present; question and conclusion extracted from letter body. Document dated February 10, 1982, responding to inquiry forwarded by Alice Hughes. References Government Code sections 82030, 82047, and 87207(b). Includes footnotes regarding conflict of interest codes and statutory references.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Must an official report the name of a lender to a business entity on their Statement of Economic Interests, and under what conditions?

CONCLUSION: An official must report a lender's name on their Statement of Economic Interests if the official has a direct or indirect interest of 10% or more in the business entity AND the official's pro rata share of the loan is $10,000 or more. The loan should be reported on Schedule H (Form 721) or Schedule G (Form 730), and the pro rata share of the loan income must also be reported on Schedule D.

Under the Political Reform Act, the term income includes all payments received, including loans. In addition, the pro rata share of any income of a business entity in which an official has a 10 percent or greater interest is income to the official,² within the meaning of the Act. Government Code Section 82030.² Thus a pro rata share both of the total gross income of a busi- ness entity in which an official has a 10 percent or greater interest, and of any loan or other payment to the business entity, is income to the official. An official has to report his or her pro rata share of income to a business entity in two ways. First, the official has to report a pro rata share of the total gross income (including _______________________________ 1/If the official is filing under a conflict of interest code, I am assuming that investments in and income from this type of business entity is reportable under the Code. 2/All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise stated. Harvey Levine, City Attorney February 10, 1982 Page Two loans) of the business entity as income on Schedule D. Second, under Section 87207(b), the official has to report the name of each person who makes payment to a business entity if the official's pro rata share of the payment is $10,000 or more. Since the term "payment" is explicitly defined to include a loan, Section 82030, and since the term "person" includes institutions, such as banks, which make loans, Section 82047, an official has to report a loan to a business entity under this section if his or her pro rata share of the loan is $10,000 or more. This should be reported on Schedule H by officials filing Form 721, and on Schedule G by officials filing Form 730. I hope this answers your questions. embedding.summary: FPPC Chief Milman responds to City Attorney Levine regarding reportability of loans to business entities, clarifying that lender names and pro rata shares of loans must be reported when an official has a 10%+ interest and their share exceeds $10,000.
